coredata.dc:title: Tailor-designed carbon-based novel fluorescent architecture for nanomolar detection of radioactive elements U(VI) and Th(IV) in pH ± 5.0
coredata.dc:creator: Shilpa, A.S.
Thangadurai, T. Daniel
Bhalerao, G.M.
Maji, Siuli
coredata.dc:description: Highly stable nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQD) functionalized with Pamoic Acid (PA@N-GQD) are utilized for nanomolar detection of radioactive elements, Uranium (VI) and Thorium (IV), in pH ± 5.0. The absorption, fluorescence, crystalline nature, elemental composition, functional groups, and morphological state of as-prepared PA@N-GQD are evaluated by UV–visible absorption, photoluminescence, XRD, XPS, FTIR, HRTEM, FESEM, and AFM characterizations. The aqueous solution of PA@N-GQD is characterized by its spherical morphology, averaging 6.5 nm in size. PA@N-GQD exhibits a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity at 438 nm (λ ex 344 nm) upon the addition of Uranium (VI) and Thorium (IV) ions. The selectivity, sensitivity, competitivity, pH, time effect, and reversibility studies of PA@N-GQDs have been carried out using the photoluminescence technique. The attained fluorescence Limit of Detection (LoD) of PA@N-GQD for Uranium (VI) and Thorium (IV) ions are 2.009 × 10−9 and 1.351 × 10−9 M, respectively. From the fluorescence titration studies of U(VI) and Th(IV), the binding constant, Stern-Volmer constant, Modified Stern-Volmer constant, association constant, and dissociation constants have been calculated separately. These aforementioned results indicate that the PA@N-GQD has a higher binding affinity towards Th(IV) than U(VI) in aqueous medium. This current research represents the development of advanced materials for environmental and analytical applications, specifically focusing on the precise detection and quantification of radioactive elements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence spectra illustrating (a) excitation spectrum of N-GQD and PA@N-GQD, and (b) emission spectrum of N-GQD and PA@N-GQD (slit width 2 nm).

(a) Fluorescence emission changes in selectivity studies of PA@N-GQD (50 × 10−6 M) with La3+ (LaCl3), Ce3+ (CeCl3), Pr3+ (PrCl3), Nd3+(NdCl3), Dy3+(DyCl3), U6+(UO2(NO3)2.6H2O) and Th4+(Th(NO3)4) (100 × 10−6 M) (slit width 2 nm; λ ex: 344 nm) in pH ± 5; (b) Corresponding bar diagram of selectivity.

Fluorescence emission changes of PA@N-GQD (50 × 10−6 M) upon addition of (a) U(VI) ions in (0 → 100 μM) in H2O (slit width 2 nm; λ ex: 344 nm) in pH ± 5; (c) Th(IV) ions in (0 → 100 μM) in H2O (slit width 2 nm; λ ex: 344 nm) in pH ± 5; (b and d) Corresponding linear fit of U(VI) and Th(IV) ions.
Stern-Volmer plot of (a) U(VI) ions and (b) Th(IV) ions. The linear fit parameters for the corresponding datasets are available in Datasheet SI3 and Datasheet SI4.
Modified Stern-Volmer plot of (a) U(VI) ions and (b) Th(IV) ions. The linear fit parameters for the corresponding datasets are available in Datasheet SI5 and Datasheet SI6.
Scatchard plot of (a) U(VI) ions and (b) Th(IV) ions. The linear fit parameters for the corresponding datasets are available in Datasheet SI7 and Datasheet SI8.

Fluorescence emission changes in reversibility test of PA@N-GQD (50 × 10−6 M) with (a) U(VI) (100 × 10−6 M) and (c) Th(IV) (100 × 10−6 M) (slit width 2 nm; λ ex: 344 nm) in pH ± 5; (b and d) Corresponding reversible cycle.
Radioactive metal ion sensing of PA@N-GQD.
Calculated Binding Parameters of PA@N-GQD upon interaction with U(VI) and Th(IV) ions in pH ± 5.
Various materials and techniques are used for the sensing of U(VI) and Th(IV) so for.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly stable nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQD) functionalized with Pamoic Acid (PA@N-GQD) are utilized for nanomolar detection of radioactive elements, Uranium (VI) and Thorium (IV), in pH ± 5.0. The absorption, fluorescence, crystalline nature, elemental composition, functional groups, and morphological state of as-prepared PA@N-GQD are evaluated by UV–visible absorption, photoluminescence, XRD, XPS, FTIR, HRTEM, FESEM, and AFM characterizations. The aqueous solution of PA@N-GQD is characterized by its spherical morphology, averaging 6.5 nm in size. PA@N-GQD exhibits a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity at 438 nm (λ ex 344 nm) upon the addition of Uranium (VI) and Thorium (IV) ions. The selectivity, sensitivity, competitivity, pH, time effect, and reversibility studies of PA@N-GQDs have been carried out using the photoluminescence technique. The attained fluorescence Limit of Detection (LoD) of PA@N-GQD for Uranium (VI) and Thorium (IV) ions are 2.009 × 10−9 and 1.351 × 10−9 M, respectively. From the fluorescence titration studies of U(VI) and Th(IV), the binding constant, Stern-Volmer constant, Modified Stern-Volmer constant, association constant, and dissociation constants have been calculated separately. These aforementioned results indicate that the PA@N-GQD has a higher binding affinity towards Th(IV) than U(VI) in aqueous medium. This current research represents the development of advanced materials for environmental and analytical applications, specifically focusing on the precise detection and quantification of radioactive elements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Thorium(Th) and Uranium(U) are heavy metals that are found naturally within the earth's crust, rock, soil, and water [1]. Th and U are also commonly found in natural waters at concentrations ranging from low nanograms (10−9 g) to picograms (10−12 g) per milliliter [2]. As per the World Health Organization (WHO) guidelines, the concentration of U and Th should not exceed 15 and 246 μg/L, respectively, in drinking water [3]. U, Th, and their derivatives are extremely hazardous to humans and animals as they lead to kidney injury and are regarded as possible cancer-causing agents [4]. U exposure can cause acute arterial lesions, kidney failure, health problems, and even death [5,6]. The act of inhaling U compounds results in the accumulation of U within the lungs, with subsequent transportation to the kidneys through the bloodstream [7]. Similarly, exposure to Th ions may result in multiple health risks, including an increased chance of lung disease, bone and pancreatic cancer, genetic body cell impairment, diffuse dermatitis, formation of deep skin cracks, and other risks [7,8]. Therefore, it is essential to determine the presence of U and Th in water.

Numerous analytical methods are utilized for the detection of U(VI)/Th(IV), including inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES), voltammetry, alpha spectrometry, ion chromatography (IC), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), capillary zone electrophoresis (CZE), and liquid scintillation [1,2]. Despite having good sensitivity and quick measurement capabilities, these above mentioned methods need expensive equipment and intricate sample pretreatment processes. Nevertheless, spectrophotometry remains an integral method for the detection of U (VI)/Th (IV) due to inexpensive, easy-to-handle, and its ability to identify a wide range of chemical sensing molecules with high affinity to form complexes with metal ions [1]. The strong affinity of carboxyl groups for various metal ions makes it challenging to selectively identify the uranyl ion among competing ions in natural environments. Therefore, achieving specific complex formation or detecting the uranyl ion amid these interfering ions continues to be an essential and challenging task [9].

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) belong to an innovative family of fluorescent zero-dimensional (0D) carbon compounds that have attracted an enormous amount of research interest owing to their distinct properties which include low cytotoxicity, chemical inertness, photobleaching, biocompatibility, cell membrane permeability, large surface area, and high conductivity [10,11]. The aforementioned features have contributed to making GQDs a versatile material that is extremely promising for a wide range of applications, including sensing, bio-imaging, nanomedicine, energy conversion, single electron transistors, spintronics, memory, and more [12]. Interestingly, GQDs have immense potential in the area of sensing because of quantum confinement, tunable electronic band structure, and edge effects. Additionally, heteroatom doping enables the tuning of the intrinsic characteristics of GQDs [13]. GQDs have superior photoluminescence properties, stability, a simple synthesis method, and the potential to functionalize their surface groups [14,15]. In GQDs surface, hydrogen and oxygen mainly exist in the form of carboxyl, epoxy, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups [16]. The GQD surface can be altered with oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, etc., and introducing various functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxylic, thiol, and amine groups significantly alters the GQD's surface, thereby increasing the number of active sites [17]. However, the heteroatoms, for example, nitrogen, on the surface of GQDs can enhance luminescence and electrical conductivity by fine-tuning their electronic structures [18].

Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs) can substantially modify their electronic characteristics, generating more active sites by tuning their atomic scale, giving rise to advanced phenomena and unexpected properties [19]. Surface functionalization or modification on GQDs is a favorable method to improve the quantum yields, intensify the luminescence, and also provide a significant intermediate for the subsequent functionalization [20]. Pamoic Acid (PA) [4,4′-methylene-bis(3-hydroxy-2-naphthalene carboxylic acid)], is a dinaphthoic acid derivative which is also referred to as embonic acid and functions as a fluorescent compound [21,22]. It constitutes a versatile ligand with carboxylic and phenoxy groups, offering the potential for diverse coordination modes for complex formation upon recognizing metal ions. Simultaneously, it demonstrates a combination of rigidity and flexibility, due to the ability of its naphthyl rings to rotate unrestrictedly around the methylene (–CH2–) groups. This adaptive feature allows adjusting minor alterations in the coordination environment, effectively minimizing steric hindrance [23].

Based on considering all the above insights, herein, we report the synthesis of PA functionalized N-GQD of spherical morphology with 6.5 nm in size, for spectrometric analysis of U(VI) and Th(IV) in pH ± 5. The formation of PA@N-GQD and their various features and properties are analyzed by standard physicochemical techniques. The fluorescence quenching technique has been utilized to conduct a range of studies encompassing selectivity, sensitivity, competitiveness, pH, time effects, and reversibility for PA@N-GQD separately interacting towards U(VI) and Th(IV) ions. One of the key aspects of this study is the utilization of photoluminescence techniques to investigate the sensing capabilities of the PA@N-GQD towards U(VI) and Th(IV) ions in aqueous medium. In comparison with the as-prepared N-GQD, the PA@N-GQD is highly stable as it can withstand room temperature for more than a year.

Citric Acid (CA) was purchased from Merck, while urea and NaOH were obtained from Nice Chemicals. The PA was procured from Sigma Aldrich. The deionized (DI) water sourced from a water purification system (AQUOION TBD 200, Two Bed Demineralizer), was used to prepare the experimental solutions. Uranyl Nitrate Hexahydrate, Thorium Nitrate, Lanthanum(III) Chloride, Cerium(III) Chloride, Praseodymium(III) Chloride, Neodymium(III) Chloride, Dysprosium(III) Chloride were acquired from the Radiochemistry Laboratory of IGCAR, Kalpakkam.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were captured using Cu-Kα radiation (1.56 Å) of Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer employing and the UV–visible spectra were collected utilizing a UV-1900 spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU Model) at KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore, India. The High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) images were examined using an HR-TEM (model JEM-2100F, JEOL) at MGU, Kottayam, India. The structural morphology was investigated using a Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM), by BRUDER (ZEISS SIGMA) with an EDS detector, from Coimbatore Institute of Technology, Coimbatore, India. The Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) was conducted using Nanosurf – Switzerland (Easy scan 2), at Lady Doak College, Madurai, India. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed utilizing a ULVAC-PHI5000 photoelectron spectrometer at SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai, India. Raman spectra were performed employing a LabRAM-HR Raman spectrometer (Horiba) with a 532 nm laser excitation source at SAIF, MGU, Kottayam, India. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) was carried out using IR Affinity – 1S (MIRacle - 10) at Avinashilingam Institute for Home Science and Higher Education for Women, Coimbatore, India, and Shimadzu IR Affinity model at PSG Institute of Advanced Studies, Coimbatore, India. The fluorescence studies were performed using an Edinburg FLS920 spectrofluorimeter with a 450W Xenon lamp as an excitation source, at the Radiochemistry Laboratory, Indira Gandhi Center for Atomic Research, Kalpakkam, India.

N-GQDs are synthesized via the hydrothermal method [24]. 2.0 g of CA and 1.9 g of urea were dissolved in 50 mL of DI water followed by stirring for 10 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the suspension was transferred into a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor and maintained at 160 °C for 4 h in a muffle furnace. Finally, the obtained green solution was filtered using a PVDF syringe filter and utilized in the process of functionalization.

An aqueous PA stock solution was prepared by dissolving 7.9 mg of PA with dilute NaOH (40 μL, 1 M) in 10 mL of water under a sonication process for 30 min. From the stock solution, 5 mL of aqueous PA solution was taken in a 50 mL beaker and freshly prepared N-GQD was gradually added dropwise into the above solution under continuous sonication for 15 min. The obtained solution was further stirred for 2 h at 40 °C. The final brown product was highly stable at room temperature for more than a year.

Nuclear research and technology advancements have led to the substantial integration of nuclear elements within various sectors [25]. Numerous fields have benefited significantly from the use of nuclear technology, including the electricity sector, military, medicine, agriculture, and other scientific research fields, while many more are still being investigated as a part of the nuclear and atomic energy program [26,27]. U and Th play crucial roles in atomic and nuclear energy programs [26]. Nuclear reactors use Th as a fuel source and find application in various industrial operations [8]. The U that remains after extracting the enriched portion is commonly known as depleted U, which can also originate from reprocessing of spent nuclear reactor fuel. Nuclear power plants are the main users of U, and most of their reactors demand U which has been elevated from 0.72% to around 3% in 235U [28]. U and Th, found in rocks, soil, water, and other sources, can enter the human food chain through multiple pathways, with water consumption being the most direct route of intake [29]. Since both U and Th are toxic to humans and animals and hazardous to the environment even in very petite concentrations, their monitoring and determination are vital to reduce their dispense into the environment in terms of economy and safety [4,26]. Therefore, for the determination of these radioactive elements in an aqueous solution, PA@N-GQD was synthesized and characterized. scheme SI1 represents the schematic formation of PA@N-GQD.

The XRD pattern of PA@N-GQD was examined to determine its surface structure in comparison with N-GQD (Figure SI1). The 2θ broad diffraction peak of N-GQD centered at 30.04°, corresponds to the graphitic structure's plane of (002) (JCPDS Card No. 75–0444) (Figure SI1a) [30]. After functionalizing N-GQD with PA, the 2θ broad diffraction peak of PA@N-GQD is centered at 30.42° and 41.87°, which exhibits the graphitic nature of plane (002) and the presence of carboxylic acid of the plane (100) (Figure SI1b) [31]. The interlayer d spacing for PA@N-GQD is 2.93 Å, whereas, in N-GQD, it's 2.97 Å, showcasing a substantial reduction in interlayer spacing as a result of their constructive π−π stacking of graphene layers with fewer surface defects [32]. This is beneficial for transporting continuous and quick ion/electron transfer channels [33]. Noticeably, after functionalizing the N-GQD with PA, the diffraction peak's intensity was reduced and the d spacing of the interlayer has also decreased. However, there is no major change in the graphitic plane, and also it exhibits more compact interlayer spacing which is similar to N-GQD.

The HRTEM images of PA@N-GQD and N-GQD were investigated to evaluate their crystallographic structure at the atomic scale. The PA@N-GQD exhibits a spherical morphology with a typical particle size of ca. 6.5 nm, whereas N-GQD shows a spherical morphology with an average diameter of 6 nm, which is displayed in Figures SI2(a) and SI2(d), respectively. The addition of PA onto the surface of N-GQDs potentially leads to a 0.5 nm increase in particle size. The HRTEM images of PA@N-GQD revealed the presence of two distinct fringe patterns, having a calculated average d-spacing value of 0.291 and 0.402 nm (Figure SI2c), whereas the calculated average d-spacing of N-GQD is 0.207 nm (Figure SI2f). So, the results suggest that the d spacing values of PA@N-GQD are higher than the N-GQD. The SAED pattern displays two planes (002) and (100), for PA@N-GQD (Figure SI2b), aligning with the XRD planes observed in PA@N-GQD. In contrast, the SAED pattern of N-GQD (Figure SI2e) exhibits a single plane, the (002) plane, which is consistent with the XRD plane observed in N-GQD. From the SAED image of PA@N-GQD, it is visible that the particles are evenly placed in the plane, which illustrates its crystalline nature. Images of PA@N-GQD and N-GQD captured through HR-TEM at various magnifications indicate the formation of quantum dots.

The FESEM is used to observe the structure of PA@N-GQD as shown in Figure SI3(a & b). The surface morphology of PA@N-GQD looks like layers of flakes in a disordered manner, whereas N-GQD has spherical morphology [34]. The energy-dispersive X-ray (EDAX) (Figure SI4) and elemental mapping (Figure SI3(c to f)) confirm the chemical composition of PA@N-GQD as C 59.81%, O 24.03%, and N 16.16% These results confirm the presence of all expected elements, affirming the high purity of the PA@N-GQD. It also demonstrates the successful attachment of PA molecules to N-GQDs and confirms the formation of PA@N-GQDs. The AFM study reveals the topology of PA@N-GQD (Figure SI5). The Roughness Average (Ra) of PA@N-GQD was obtained as 7.34 nm and shows Maximum Profile Peak Height (Rp) at 20.65 nm. The average height of PA@N-GQD is 7.34 nm which is higher than that of N-GQD (1–5 nm) by more than ca. 2.34 nm [35].

The XPS analysis of PA@N-GQD was evaluated to determine the elemental composition and chemical bonding. The XPS survey spectrum of PA@N-GQD exhibits C 1s at 284.99 eV, N 1s at 399.94 eV, and O 1s at 531.99 eV (Figure SI6a). According to quantitative analysis, a distribution of 66.5 at.% of carbon, 26.1 at.% of oxygen, and 7.4 at.% of nitrogen was identified in PA@N-GQD (Figure SI6a inset). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of PA@N-GQD displayed three characteristic peaks at 284.60, 286.22, and 288.39 eV which represent CC, O–CC, and CO, respectively (Figure SI6b) [34]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum displays two characteristic peaks at 399.56 and 400.46 eV, indicating the type of nitrogen that is bounded in the N-GQD which is categorized as CN, and the pyrrolic N group on the PA@N-GQDs surface, which is in accordance with N-GQDs (Figure SI6c) [34,36]. The decrease in the atomic percentage of nitrogen-to-carbon (0.1113) implies that PA molecules are attached to the surface of N-GQD in a monodentate manner [27]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.65 and 533.33 eV, which corresponds to the -O–H and C–OH bonds present in the PA@N-GQD, respectively (Figure SI6d) [37]. The low atomic percent of oxygen-to-carbon (0.3924) ratio aligns with the high-purity graphene layers [30]. These XPS characteristics peaks confirm the formation of PA@N-GQD further and comply with the FTIR spectrum.

Raman spectra analysis has been conducted to evaluate the structural defects in PA@N-GQD in comparison with as-prepared N-GQD. The D band in the Raman spectrum is linked with sp3-hybridized carbon atoms arising from defects and oxygen functional groups that are covalently bonded with the graphitic structure. The G band originates from sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. It is well known that, when the G band is narrower there are only fewer functional groups present on the surface of the sample. The Raman spectrum of PA@N-GQD (Figure SI7a) exhibits the D and G bands at 1348 and 1561 cm−1 whereas N-GQDs exhibit at 1347 and 1575 cm−1 (Figure SI7b), respectively [25,33]. After functionalization of N-GQD with PA, there is a soft broadening in the bands, along with a slight blue shift (1 cm−1) in the D band, and a significant red shift (14 cm−1) in the G band is observed for PA@N-GQD. This peak broadening is primarily caused by the increased dispersion of bond stretching frequencies, which is brought by the inclusion of hydroxyl and epoxy groups. These groups also contribute to the increase in the D band intensity by disrupting the lattice's symmetry [38]. The slight shift in the D and G bands is induced due to the electrical interactions between PA and the N-GQDs as well as the modest deformation of sp2 groups at the corners of N-GQDs upon functionalization [10]. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of PA@N-GQD is 0.95, while for N-GQD it is 0.60. This high-intensity ratio in PA@N-GQD suggests that multiple structural defects were induced by the N atoms which are intercalated into the conjugated carbon backbone [39]. In comparison with N-GQD, the structure is more defined in PA@N-GQD with fewer defects.

The FT-IR spectrum reveals the bonding arrangements and functional groups present in PA@N-GQDs upon functionalizing of N-GQD with PA. In N-GQD, the O–H and N–H vibrational stretching was observed around 3240 cm−1 confirming the existence of hydroxyl and amino groups, reinforcing the hydrophilic characteristic of GQD (Figure SI8a). The two distinct peaks that appeared at 1635 and 1458 cm−1 are associated with the carbonyl group CO and the C–H vibrations, respectively, indicating the successful surface passivation reaction of GQDs [34]. Additionally, other bands corresponding to the C–N stretching vibrations and CC vibrations are observed at 1080 and 671 cm−1. Upon functionalizing the N-GQDs with PA, a new CN vibrational stretching band became visible at 1653 cm−1. The formation of an imine bond confirms the functionalization of N-GQD with PA (Figure SI8b). The appearance of new O–H stretching vibrations at 3643, 3294, 3045, and 2579 cm−1, the frequency shift of C–H bending vibrations at 1975 and 1838 cm−1, the CCO stretching at 2146 cm−1 and C–N stretching at 1207 cm−1 indicates the formation of PA@N-GQD. The FTIR findings correlate effectively with the outcomes derived from XPS.

The UV–visible studies were conducted to determine the optical properties of PA@N-GQD (Figure SI9b). A pronounced absorption peak was observed for PA@N-GQD at 236 nm (for N-GQD it is 234 nm), which corresponds to the sp2 hybridized orbitals of C–C and CC (π→π* transition). A prominent absorption peak appeared at 338 nm for PA@N-GQD, similar to N-GQD, due to the sp3 hybridized orbitals of CO (n→π* transition) [40]. In π→π* transition, a redshift (∼2 nm) was observed for PA@N-GQD, in comparison with N-GQD, which is due to extended conjugation of PA bound to the surface of N-GQDs. The presence of a small hump centered at 455 nm in PA@N-GQD depicts the formation of the imine (-CN-) bond which is formed through the condensation reaction between carbonyl (CO) of PA and NH2 of N-GQD (Figure SI9a). This observation is consistent with findings from both XPS and FTIR spectra.

The Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of N-GQD displays an excitation wavelength of 309 and 366 nm, whereas PA@N-GQD exhibits an excitation of 344 nm (Fig. 1 a). The position of this excitation band of PA@N-GQD is at the center with respect to the two absorption maxima of N-GQD. These changes in the excitation wavelength of PA@N-GQD might be due to the formation of an imine bond between the carboxylic group of PA molecule and the amine group of N-GQD, which is in correlation with FT-IR spectra. In other words, since PA establishes chemical bonding with N-GQD, PA@N-GQD becomes a single system, a complex, having an excitation maximum at 344 nm, and its broadness extends from 290 to 390 nm which is similar to N-QGD. The excitation and emission spectrum of PA molecules were given in the supporting information (Figure SI10a and SI10b, respectively). Noteworthy to mention that PA is a fluorescent molecule [22]. The PL emission spectra of N-GQD and PA@N-GQD exhibit an intense emission peak at 438 nm (λ ex 366 and 344 nm, respectively; slit width 2 nm each) (Fig. 1b). Interstingly, the amine (-NH2) and carboxylic (-COOH) functional groups linked to the N-GQD and PA have electron-donating and withdrawing properties, respectively [41]. Therefore, as we functionalize N-GQD with PA, the amine group donates the electrons to the carboxylic group due to which there is a decrease in the emission fluorescence intensity of PA@N-GQD when compared with N-GQD.

By using this fluorescence emission nature of PA@N-GQD, the analytes, U (VI) and Th (IV) ions, are detected through the quenching mechanism. Various PL studies such as selectivity, sensitivity, competitivity, pH, time effect, and reversibility analysis were also carried out to determine the strong binding affinity of PA@N-GQD with these radioactive elements when compared with the lanthanides.

To validate the selectivity of PA@N-GQD towards U(VI) and/or Th(IV), a selectivity study with chloride salts of lanthanides such as Lanthanum (La3+), Cerium (Ce3+), Praseodymium (Pr3+), Neodymium (Nd3+), Dysprosium (Dy3+), and Uranyl nitrate hexahydrate and Thorium nitrate in aqueous solution was carried out (Fig. 2 ). The fluorescence emission intensity of PA@N-GQD undergoes an approximate ca. 9-fold decrease upon the addition of Th(IV). However, the fluorescence emission of PA@N-GQD was decreased only by a ca. 2-fold upon the addition of U(VI) ions. Figure SI11 shows the comparative analysis of the quenching percentage of PA@N-GQD with various lanthanides (Ce3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, and Dy3+) and radioactive elements (U6+ and Th4+). PA@N-GQD shows the highest quenching efficiency percentage of 86.25% with Th(IV) followed by 40.50% with U(VI). Though La3+ showed fluorescence enhancement, this fluorescence selectivity result led us to consider the detection of both Th(IV) and U(VI) in an aqueous solution at pH ± 5.

The limit of detection (LoD) was estimated by plotting a linear fit of Fo/Fo-F against 1/[Q], where “Fo” represents the initial fluorescence intensity of PA@N-GQD, and “F” represents the fluorescence intensity after the addition of U(VI) and Th(IV) ions, respectively. Consequently, the established LoD for U(VI) and Th(IV) ions are 2.009 × 10−9 (Datasheet SI1) and 1.351 × 10−9 M (Datasheet SI2), respectively. This demonstrates that PA@N-GQD can detect U(VI) and Th(IV) ions at the nanomolar level with PA@N-GQD exhibiting a lower LoD for Th(IV) ions. From these results, we can conclude from the fluorescence titration experiment that PA@N-GQD is a highly stable efficient fluorescent sensor for U(VI) and Th(VI) ions in an aqueous solution at pH ± 5. Scheme 1 represents the binding interaction of radioactive elements U(VI) and Th(IV) ions with PA@N-GQD.

The Stern-Volmer equation (eq. (1)) [42] interprets the quenching in the emission intensity of fluorescence observed in PA@N-GQD upon the addition of U(VI) and Th(IV) ions.
(eq. 1)

where ‘Ksv’ stands for the Stern-Volmer constant, ‘Fo’ and ‘F’ denote the fluorescence intensity of PA@N-GQD in the absence and presence of the analyte, and [Analyte] signifies the concentration of the analyte (U(VI) or Th(IV) ions). The Stern-Volmer constant of PA@N-GQD with U(VI) and Th(IV) ions were determined using the Stern-Volmer equation, by graphing Fo/F against the concentration of analyte (Fig. 4 ). The interaction between PA@N-GQD and U(VI) ions (Ksv = 8.99 × 10−3 LM−1) is responsible for the linear Stern-Volmer quenching relationship, which suggests the occurrence of either static or dynamic quenching (Fig. 4a) (Datasheet SI3). The interaction of Th(IV) ions with PA @ N-GQD is the mechanism that causes the polynomial curve of Stern-Volmer quenching, indicating the combination of both static and dynamic quenching (Fig. 4b) (Datasheet SI4) [43].

The modified Stern-Volmer equation (eq. (2)) [43] provides a clear explanation for the fluorescence quenching observed in PA@N-GQD as a result of U(VI) and Th(IV) ions (Fig. 5 ).

where “Ka” represents the binding constant. The derived Ka values for U(VI) ions (8.63 × 10−3 L/mol) (Datasheet SI5) and Th(IV) ions 1.749 × 10−2 L/mol (Datasheet SI6) (Datasheet SI6)) signify a stronger binding affinity of PA@N-GQD with Th(IV) ions in comparison to U(VI) ions.

The association constant (Kass) and the binding sites (n) on PA@N-GQDs surface involved in the interaction of U(VI) and Th(IV) ions were employed from the Scatchard plot analysis (Fig. 6 ) (eq. (3)) [44].

versus log Analyte. The linear Scatchard plot yielded association constants (Kass) of 3.312 × 10−3 (Datasheet SI7) for U(VI) ions and 4.33 × 10−3 M−1 (Datasheet SI8) for Th(IV) ions. Additionally, the binding site “n” value of one suggests that PA@N-GQD binds in a 1:1 ratio towards U(VI) and Th(IV) ions separately.

To determine whether the interaction between PA@N-GQD and U(VI), as well as PA@N-GQD and Th(IV), exhibits cooperative or non-cooperative binding due to the potential for multiple metal ion interactions, a Hill plot was employed (eq. (4)) [45]. This graphical method assists in identifying the nature of the interaction and facilitates the calculation of binding parameters.

Where “K d” stands for the dissociation constant, “θ” denotes the portion of active sites on PA@N-GQD engaged by U(VI) and Th(IV) ions. From the equation, “nH” signifies the Hill coefficient, and “[Analyte]” represents the concentration of the analyte (U(VI) or Th(IV) ions). The Hill coefficient value obtained from the experiments indicates the favorable binding site on the surface of PA@N-GQD, for U(VI) ions (nH = 1) and Th(IV) ions (nH = 1). The Hills plot illustrates PA@N-GQD's interaction with U(VI) ions, displaying a high dissociation constant (K d = 2.814 × 102 M−1) (Datasheet SI9) alongside 1:1 stoichiometry (Figure SI12a). Conversely, PA@N-GQD interacts with Th(IV) in a single possible manner, exhibiting a lower dissociation constant (K d = 1.884 × 102 M−1) (Datasheet SI10) (Figure SI12b). The analysis from the Hill plot distinctly outlines the presence of cooperative binding between PA@N-GQD and Th(IV) ions and non-cooperative binding between PA@N-GQD and U(VI) ions.

Through fluorescence titration studies, we derived binding constants, Stern-Volmer constants, modified Stern-Volmer constants, association constants, and dissociation constants to study the binding of PA@N-GQD with U(VI) and Th(IV) ions separately in aqueous solutions of pH ± 5. All these obtained data are summarized in Table 1 . Analysis of these constants notably indicates a stronger binding affinity between PA@N-GQD and Th(IV) ions compared to its interaction with U(VI) ions.

Competing experiments are conducted in the presence of different lanthanides using the fluorescence approach to validate an effective interaction of PA@N-GQD towards U(VI) and Th(IV) ions in H2O separately. The lanthanides with the quantity of 2-fold (200 × 10−6 M) each including La3+ (LaCl3), Ce3+ (CeCl3), Pr3+(PrCl3), Nd3+(NdCl3) and Dy3+(DyCl3) are introduced into the PA@N-GQD (50 × 10−6 M) aqueous solution with U(VI) (100 × 10−6 M) (Figure SI13a) and Th(IV) (100 × 10−6 M) (Figure SI13c) separately. Interestingly, no noticeable variation in the intensity of the PA@N-GQD fluorescence emission was observed when intrusive lanthanides were present. It underscores that the augmented selectivity of PA@N-GQD for U(VI) and Th(IV) ions remains practically unaffected due to the presence of lanthanides. This analysis significantly validates PA@N-GQD's high selectivity and anti-interference sensing capacity for radioactive elements U(VI) and Th(IV) ions in competitive environments.

The pH dependence of PA@N-GQD (50 × 10−6 M) with U(VI) (100 × 10−6 M) (Figure SI14a) and Th(IV) (100 × 10−6 M) (Figure SI14c) is studied separately in H2O, from pH 1 (acidic) to pH 13 (basic). Under highly acidic conditions, there is a significant decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity of the solution. This is due to the protonation of the Schiff-base group within PA@N-GQD hinders the strong binding of U(VI) and Th(IV) ions as the pH falls below 3. Consequently, as the pH increases from 3 to 5, the fluorescence intensity of PA@N-GQD gradually increases. However, at the basic conditions from pH 7 to 13, there are no apparent relative changes in the fluorescent intensity with respect to PA@N-GQD. This suggests that the binding affinity of PA@N-QGD towards U(VI) and Th(IV) ions is likely to be lower under basic conditions. Therefore, PA@N-GQD displays a capacity for detecting radioactive elements effectively at pH ± 5. The PA@N-GQD is highly stable at room temperature with a pH of approximately 5. Even upon the addition of micromolar concentrations (100 μM) of U(VI) and Th(IV) ions into PA@N-GQD, the pH of the solution consistently maintains its level at 5. It confirms that the as-prepared PA@NGQD is sensitive to pH which means that the original PL emission from this system is dominated by the functional groups attached to the surface. Since these functional groups are sensitive to pH, we have noticed a pH-dependent PL emission intensity change [46]. These aforementioned results suggest that the optimized pH of 5 is suitable for fluorescence deduction for U(VI) and Th(IV) ions.

The effects of time on the fluorescence emission of PA@N-GQD (50 × 10−6 M) in the presence of U(VI) and Th(IV) ions of (100 × 10−6 M), in H2O separately, were investigated at every 5 min interval up to 30 min. The PA@N-GQD undergoes a 60% fluorescence quenching within a minute upon the addition of U(VI) at 438 nm (λ ex: 344 nm) (Figure SI15a). As the duration progresses to 30 min, there is an additional 40% fluorescence quenching compared to the initial time. However, the PA@N-GQD undergoes an immediate 99% of their fluorescence quenching within a minute upon the addition of Th(IV) at 438 nm (λ ex: 344 nm) (Figure SI15c). As the duration progresses to 30 min, there will be an additional 1% fluorescence quenching compared to the initial minute. From this study, we can interpret a strong binding interaction of PA@N-GQD towards Th(IV) rather than U(VI).

The reversibility test was performed to corroborate the fluorescence sensing nature of PA@N-GQD. The emission spectrum of PA@N-GQD (50 × 10−6 M) was restored by the addition of EDTA to the solution mixture of PA@N-GQD and U(VI) (100 × 10−6 M) (Fig. 7 a) and PA@N-GQD and Th(IV) (100 × 10−6 M) (Fig. 7c). Additionally, upon reintroducing U(VI) and Th(IV) into a mixture of a solution containing PA@N-GQD, U(VI), EDTA, and PA@N-GQD, Th(IV), EDTA, the reversibility cycles did not develop. Only a minor visible fluorescence quenching was observed signifying a lack of full reversibility during the later stages of the cycle. The increase of the fluorescence intensity relative to the quenching of PA@N-GQD with U(VI) and Th(IV), might be due to an excess of EDTA that remained in the solution hindering the effective complexation of PA@N-GQD and U(VI) and Th(IV).

Based on the experiments conducted, it is evident that PA@N-GQD exhibits a stronger binding affinity with Th in comparison to U. This finding suggests that PA@N-GQD may have potential applications in the selective extraction or separation of Th over U in relevant chemical processes or environmental remediation efforts for various scientific or industrial purposes. Considering our extensive review of the literature and to the best of our knowledge, there are only three reports (entries 1, 8, and 9 in Table 2 ) for the detection of U(VI) and Th(IV) available to date [47–52]. Among those three reports, the current research (entry 9 in Table 2) represents the inaugural study in which PA-functionalized N-GQD have been employed for the rapid detection of U(VI) and Th(IV) with very little LoD in comparison with the other two reports (entry 1 and 8 in Table 2). The entries (2–7 in Table 2) showed the detection of either U(VI) or Th(IV) by using various detection techniques.

The PA-functionalized N-GQDs were successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal method using CA and urea as precursors. The performed comprehensive array of characterization techniques confirms the formation of PA@N-GQD in spherical morphology, averaging 6.5 nm in size. The PA@N-GQD was used for the detection of U(VI) and Th(IV) ions quantitatively at the nanomolar concentration in aqueous solutions at pH ± 5 using the photoluminescence technique. The limit of detection of U(VI) and Th(IV) is found to be 2.009 × 10−9 and 1.351 × 10−9 M, respectively, indicating that PA@N-GQD exhibits low LoD specifically for Th(IV) ions. These obtained LoD values are much lesser than the permissible concentration of U and Th in drinking water (15 and 246 μg/L, respectively) as per the WHO guidelines, and also adequately low for the detection of submicromolar concentration ranges of U and Th in various relevant processes. The calculated Stern-Volmer, binding constant, association, and dissociation constants collectively point out that, Th(IV) exhibits a stronger binding affinity with PA@N-GQD than U(VI). Interestingly, the PA@N-GQD binds with U(VI) in cooperative mode and with Th(IV) in a non-cooperative mode in a 1:1 stoichiometry. The PA@N-GQD displays a 60% and 99% immediate fluorescence quenching at 438 nm (λ ex: 344 nm) within a minute upon the addition of U(VI) and Th(IV), respectively. The novelty of the PA@N-GQD compound is its stability to withstand room temperature for more than one year and it consists of more binding sites to bind with the U(VI) and Th(IV) ions. Further research and exploration in this direction could yield valuable insights and practical applications. Therefore, concerning the achieved results, the development of an on-field detection device for U(VI) and Th(IV) is currently underway with the collaboration of IGCAR, India, and the outcomes will be published in due course.
